text: This is a sixty-five-year-old male with an incarcerated left inguinal scrotal hernia. He was sent to me by the gastroenterologist because his CAT scan showed a large incarcerated inguinal scrotal hernia containing his sigmoid colon.
They weren't able to do his CAT scan, so they needed him to have this repaired prior.
The surgeon begins to access the preperitoneal pocket, preparing to manipulate the tissue without taking the colon down.
The surgeon opens the flap and lifts it to inspect the underlying area, ensuring the colon is intact and not compromised.
The surgeon extends the medial dissection further than usual for large inguinal scrotal hernias, enhancing visualization by taking the flap down more to expose both sides of the defect.
The surgeon observes the chronic hernia sac, which is visibly fused to the ilio-pubic tract below.
The surgeon observes a very thickened hernia sac, noting its thickness compared to an average sac, indicating the hernia has been present for several years.
The surgeon sweeps surrounding tissue away to reveal the thickened hernia sac, while searching for the vas deferens within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the colon positioned lower in the surgical field, ensuring it remains unaffected during the procedure.

The surgeon navigates around the corner, revealing a substantial amount of adipose tissue over the iliac vessels. The presence of a clear tissue plane is assessed to determine whether the fat can be preserved.
The surgeon picks up the thickened hernia sac at multiple locations, revealing small filmy attachments that are visually distinct from other areas without such attachments.
The surgeon places a mesh over the hernia site, ensuring it fits perfectly. The mesh is fixed laterally, positioned high above the myopectineal orifice.
The surgeon positions the mesh medial to the main coronavorous vein, ensuring it curls around the triangle of doom to prevent recurrence.
The surgeon performs a supermedial fixation as part of the standard closure technique, securing the mesh in place within the surgical field.
